reasoning: The historical data from FRED reveals a seasonal pattern or a tendency for the series to revert to a higher mean after sharp dips. The 1-year expected inflation rate dips sharply in March before rebounding significantly in April and remaining elevated through most of the year. For instance, the value was 2.08 in March 2024 before jumping to 2.70 in April 2024 and remaining robustly above 2.20 through the rest of the year. Given that the current value around March 25 is roughly 2.18, this low base establishes a very high likelihood for an increase in the short term (April and June resolution dates), as nearly all other months in the recent year were substantially higher. As the horizon extends to September, the value typically cools moderately but remains above the March low, yet overall long-term downward trends in structural inflation limit the ceiling, bringing the probability somewhat closer to 0.5. By March 2026, the question will compare another expected seasonal low point against the current low, making it highly uncertain whether long-term disinflation will drive next year's March expectations below the 2.18 mark, thus pushing the probability very close to a coin flip as horizon uncertainty maximizes.
